# Extração: 04-The BPM Method - Nitro V-Scale

- **Fonte:** 04-The BPM Method - Nitro V-Scale.docx

Nitro V-Scale Guide



Use this alongside the training video to implement this strategy.



This is an ADVANCED scaling strategy so use with caution and at your own risk.



Once you’ve found stability with your Prospecting campaigns over a period of at least 7 days and you’re confident you have a strong audience, with a good size (1M+), a strong funnel and backup ads (for ad fatigue which happens when you scale) you can test larger budget bumps

If you’re worried about the ‘learning phase’ there are 2 things to consider:

The more history you’ve built up, in spend and conversions within your ad set (in particular consistently getting well over the 50 conversion events per week - and over 200 for the lifetime - per ad set) the more likely the learning phase won’t matter as much. Because your audience is strong, conversions high and Facebook knows your prime user, your ad set will survive going back into learning phase and come back as before (potentially with a small negative impact but your volume should be able to ride it out)

We’ve found on some occasions resetting the Learning Phase to be a good thing at scale, when performance has been getting worse, by replacing ads, dropping budgets and forcing the system to rethink often kick-starts things especially if the audience saturation is high. This is also why duplicating a previously good performing ad set can be a way of kickstarting results as Facebook has a way of getting caught in a smaller net of users in your audience that saturate and performance drops.

When I’m confident in the above, I’d push higher budget bumps upwards of 50% to 500%, often more than once in a day

Increase your budgets after 6am no later than 12pm - any later and the Pacing Algorithm  (which uses a 24-hour clock based on the ad account timezone to plan your spend) can go a little crazy and overspend. 

I choose 6am, since resetting at midnight often brings buyers from the previous day and causes instability when increasing budgets, so 6am is a clean break of a new day. If you’d prefer to be at your laptop and 6am is too early you can go for 8 or 9am.

Ensure at this stage you’re using automated rules to minimise losses  - we use Revealbot (30% Discount for the first 6 months with code depeshmastermind) for many reasons, including checking performance every 15 minutes instead of Facebook’s 30 minute checks and its ability to push alerts into Slack which helps our process flow. However if you don’t wish to invest in a tool you can implement this into Facebook’s native rules.

Here’s how we structure the pause rules for Ecommerce:

If you’ve spent 3X your ideal CPA target at the ad level with no sales AND cost per Initiate Checkout is below 25% of your target, PAUSE AD

Up until 2pm: If you have a sale AND have spent more than 3X your ideal CPA target AND your ROAS is less than 50% of your ideal ROAS target, PAUSE AD

From 2pm-5pm: If you have a sale AND have spent more than 3X your ideal CPA target AND your ROAS is less than 20% of your ideal ROAS target, PAUSE AD

From 5pm-midnight: If you have a sale AND have spent more than 3X your ideal CPA target AND your ROAS is less than your ideal ROAS target, PAUSE AD

Note: never set and forget rules. Keep tracking them closely and make adjustments as needed. For example your sales may trickle in after 5pm and your pause rule might be too aggressive before that, so you should adapt it. Setting rules with high scaling is an advanced strategy so use with caution otherwise you could lose a lot of money, fast.

If by 3pm you have negative AD SET performance (ROAS/CPA not on track), drop CBO budgets by 10% and if they don’t recover within 2 hours, drop the spend back down to the original budget (even if it pauses everything out). The 10% drop is part of The Punisher Method which is a proven way of course correcting performance fully explained in The BPM Method program - for now, test it and thank me later.

The next morning there will be a high chance, if your budget starts considerably larger than the day before, the morning will suffer - this depends on how aggressive you’ve been so you have 2 options here:

Reset the budget the next day (using rules) at midnight to the previous day’s start budget, plus 20%. So essentially you’re still moving the needle  forward but catering for Facebook overspending in the early hours. You can then follow the high scale step above.

Leave the larger budget because you’re happy with the risk, for the chance of higher rewards.

My approach to this method is more moderate unless:

I’m in a highly seasonal period (eg Black Friday) or limited sale period and need to max out sales, fast, in which case I’d scale as aggressively as I can, pushing budgets 100-1000% depending on my base,  implement automated rules and be prepared to cycle ads as needed whilst watching my account like a hawk - with this kind of scaling I would loosen my automated rules but monitor my account more closely

I want to scale up in a more staggered way, in which case I’d test the standard V-Scale with 30% bumps over 3-7 days THEN move up to  50%, 75%, 100% whilst maintaining stable results.

This is very high risk but it does work - we would not have been able to push $7M in sales with around $950k spend without being aggressive with scale (and optimising AOV, Conversion Rate and Auction Costs) - knowing our 5W Avatar, CORE-4, Graduation Framework, GT-CBO and rules were dialed in actually made it predictable and easier for us to manage. The groundwork goes a long way to easing the scaling process.
